name: CryoPipelineQA-lite
description: A benchmark of cryo-EM single-particle-analysis processing DECISIONS, each grounded in real pipeline metrics. Tests whether an LLM reasons correctly about processing choices (not factual recall). Ground-truth answers reflect established SPA practice; several scenarios use real EMPIAR-10288 numbers from our runs. (v0.2: option positions randomized, seed=42, so the correct letter is not positionally biased.)
scoring: single-best-answer multiple choice; accuracy over items

items.scenario: After patch-CTF, exposure curation reports median CTF fit resolution 8.5 A on 40% of micrographs; the rest fit to <4 A.
items.question: What is the best action?

options.A: Reject everything and re-collect
options.B: Reject the 40% with poor CTF fit and keep the rest
options.C: Lower the defocus and re-estimate CTF only
options.D: Accept all exposures; more data is always better

items.rationale: Poor CTF fit (>~5-8 A) indicates thin-ice/drift/contamination; excluding those exposures improves downstream signal while retaining the good majority.

items.scenario: Curation acceptance rate is 96% with median CTF fit 3.1 A and uniform ice thickness.
items.question: How should the agent proceed?

options.A: Abort â€” acceptance is suspiciously high
options.C: Tighten thresholds to reject more, to be safe

items.rationale: High acceptance with good CTF fits is a healthy dataset; over-tightening would discard usable data.

items.scenario: Blob picker returns ~8 picks/micrograph on a 150 kDa soluble protein at 1.0 A/pix.
items.question: What does this most likely indicate?

options.A: The picker is missing particles or particle concentration is low

items.rationale: Well-distributed grids usually yield tens-to-hundreds of picks/micrograph; ~8/mic suggests under-picking (diameter/threshold) or sparse particles.

items.scenario: 2D classification yields several high-resolution class averages with clear secondary structure plus many blurry/streaky classes.
items.question: Best next step?

options.A: Select the sharp, high-detail classes and discard blurry/junk classes
options.B: Discard the sharp classes as overfit

items.rationale: Selecting well-resolved classes removes junk/misaligned particles before 3D, improving ab-initio and refinement.

items.scenario: A dataset of 1,735,240 particles after 2D. A fixed correlation threshold keeps only a small clean subset that refines to 3.21 A; recruiting the top 52% by reference correlation (1,280,400 particles) and re-refining gives 3.13 A.
items.question: What does this tell you about the fixed threshold?

options.A: The fixed threshold was over-restrictive and discarded usable signal
options.B: More particles always improve resolution regardless of quality
options.C: The 3.13 A result must be an error

items.rationale: Recovering resolution by adaptively recruiting more particles shows the fixed cutoff removed particles that still carried usable high-resolution signal (real EMPIAR-10288 result).

items.scenario: Recruiting particles from top 52% to top 90% by correlation degrades resolution from 3.13 A to 3.6 A.
items.question: Interpretation?

options.A: There is an optimum; beyond it added particles are mostly junk that hurts resolution

items.rationale: Adaptive selection has a sweet spot; too-loose selection admits junk/misaligned particles that add noise, motivating closed-loop tuning over a fixed cutoff.

items.scenario: Ab-initio reconstruction on 4,000 particles produces a low-resolution, noisy volume with poor angular coverage.
items.question: Most likely fix?

options.A: Skip ab-initio and go straight to polishing
options.B: Provide more clean particles (tens of thousands) for a stable ab-initio

items.rationale: Ab-initio stability needs sufficient clean particles and angular coverage; ~4k is often too few for a reliable de novo model.

items.scenario: Apoferritin is being processed but refinement is run in C1 and stalls at ~10 A on only 1,802 particles.
items.question: What are the two most relevant issues?

options.A: Apoferritin has octahedral (O) symmetry that should be applied, and the particle count is far too low
options.B: The map is fine; 10 A is expected
options.C: Wrong pixel size and too much contrast

items.rationale: Apoferritin is O-symmetric; not applying symmetry wastes signal, and ~1.8k particles is far below what high-resolution reconstruction needs (real failure mode from our run).

items.scenario: Homogeneous refinement gives 3.96 A; switching to non-uniform refinement on the same particles gives 3.21 A.
items.question: Why did non-uniform refinement help?

options.A: It used more particles
options.C: Adaptive regularization suppresses noise in disordered/solvent regions, improving alignment

items.rationale: Non-uniform refinement (Punjani 2020) applies cross-validated local filtering that improves alignment for membrane proteins/small particles.

items.scenario: GS-FSC reports 3.13 A but the FSC curve shows a sharp spike near the mask edge and the map has a flat, featureless surface.
items.question: What should the agent suspect?

options.A: The particle diameter is wrong
options.D: Mask-related overfitting / tight-mask artifact inflating the FSC

items.rationale: An FSC spike at the mask boundary with poor map features suggests mask overfitting; the reported resolution may be optimistic and needs a looser/soft mask check.

items.scenario: The agent must report the resolution of a refinement job. The job document field shows 100.0; the event/stream log shows 'Using Filter Radius ... (3.134A)'.
items.question: Which value is the true GS-FSC resolution?

options.A: Their average

items.rationale: The real GS-FSC resolution is emitted in the stream/event log; the 100.0 is a default placeholder (this was a real bug we fixed).

items.scenario: A 3.2 A GPCR map is limited by residual per-particle defocus and beam-tilt errors.
items.question: Which step most directly addresses this?

options.A: Exposure curation

items.rationale: CTF refinement corrects per-particle/per-group defocus and higher-order aberrations, often recovering resolution near the tail.

items.scenario: Reference-based motion correction (polishing) fails on a dataset whose movies have inconsistent frame counts (some 30, some 40 frames).
items.question: What is the correct diagnosis?

options.A: Polishing is never needed
options.B: Heterogeneous frame counts break the polishing assumptions; harmonize/subset the movies first
options.C: The GPU ran out of memory

items.rationale: Polishing models per-frame motion; inconsistent frame counts violate its input assumptions and cause failure (real failure we hit).

items.scenario: An autonomous pipeline reaches 3.21 A on EMPIAR-10288; the deposited structure and CryoSPARC's own benchmark reach 3.0 A on the same data.
items.question: What is the honest conclusion?

options.A: Resolution differences this small are meaningless
options.C: The pipeline is ~0.2 A worse than the reference; likely due to skipped polishing/CTF refinement

items.rationale: 3.21 vs 3.0 A is a real gap; the missing polishing/CTF-refinement steps are the likely cause and a concrete thing to close.

items.scenario: A closed-loop selector adjusts the keep-fraction from resolution feedback with a keep-best rule: if a new selection refines worse, the previous best is retained.
items.question: What safety property does keep-best guarantee?

options.A: It always reaches the target resolution
options.B: It can never report a result worse than the baseline it started from
options.C: It removes the need for refinement
options.D: It uses fewer particles each iteration

items.rationale: Keep-best is a monotone-improvement guard: the reported result is the arg-min resolution over evaluated iterations, so it is <= baseline.

items.scenario: Resolution (3.13 A) is above the target (3.0 A). The control law is theta(t+1)=clip(theta(t) - alpha*(res - target)).
items.question: In which direction does the keep-threshold move, and what is the effect?

options.A: Threshold to zero -> keep none

items.rationale: res>target makes (res-target)>0, so theta decreases, lowering the acceptance bar and recruiting more particles to add signal.

items.scenario: An LLM critic is told: achieved 3.134 A, published reference 3.0 A. It replies 'the achieved resolution is better than the published reference.'
items.question: Is the LLM's statement correct?

options.A: No â€” smaller Angstrom is better, so 3.134 A is worse than 3.0 A

items.rationale: Lower resolution value = higher detail; 3.134 A is worse than 3.0 A. This is a real LLM grounding error we observed and a target of the grounding audit.

items.scenario: 3D refinement of a complex shows smeared density for one domain while the core is sharp.
items.question: Best strategy?

options.A: Use 3D classification / local refinement to resolve compositional or conformational heterogeneity

items.rationale: Localized blurring indicates flexibility/heterogeneity; 3D classification or masked local refinement resolves distinct states/domains.

items.scenario: A membrane protein map is anisotropic with streaking along Z and the orientation distribution is concentrated in a few views.
items.question: Root cause and remedy?

options.A: Wrong symmetry
options.C: Preferred orientation; collect tilted data or add detergent/surfactant to diversify views

items.rationale: Concentrated views cause anisotropic resolution; tilt collection or grid/sample changes broaden angular coverage.

items.scenario: CryoSPARC Live already automates motion/CTF/streaming and reference-based auto-2D/3D selection.
items.question: Where does an autonomous reasoning agent add the most value?

options.A: Redoing motion correction
options.B: Strategic decisions and failure recovery on hard cases (heterogeneity, preferred orientation, novel targets) that fixed automation does not handle

items.rationale: Off-the-shelf automation handles the routine path; the agent's value is judgment/recovery on non-routine datasets where humans normally intervene.

items.scenario: Particles are extracted in a box that is only slightly larger than the particle, clipping the CTF delocalization signal.
items.question: Consequence and fix?

options.A: No effect; smaller box is fine
options.D: High-resolution CTF signal is lost; use a larger box (typically >~1.5-2x particle diameter)

items.rationale: Too-small boxes truncate delocalized high-frequency CTF signal, capping resolution; boxes should accommodate delocalization.

items.scenario: Two overlapping picks within a few Angstroms produce near-duplicate particles that survive into refinement.
items.question: Why remove duplicates?

options.A: They speed up refinement
options.D: Duplicates violate the independence assumption of GS-FSC and can inflate apparent resolution

items.rationale: Duplicate particles correlate the two half-sets, breaking gold-standard independence and biasing FSC.

items.scenario: A refinement uses a very tight mask hugging the model.
items.question: Primary risk?

options.A: Mask overfitting that inflates the reported FSC/resolution

items.rationale: Overly tight masks introduce correlations at the boundary and can produce artifactually high FSC values; soft/padded masks are safer.

items.scenario: An ACPS iteration selects 0 particles because the 3D reference is weak, and the subsequent refinement job would refine ~0 particles.
items.question: Correct autonomous behavior?

options.A: Duplicate particles to reach the minimum
options.C: Launch the refine and let it crash
options.D: Guard: detect the too-few-particles case, skip the doomed refine, and keep the best prior result

items.rationale: A minimum-particle guard prevents spawning a failing job and preserves the best result (a guard we added after a real 0-particle event).

items.scenario: Reporting the resolution of a final map for publication.
items.question: Which criterion is standard?

options.A: The nominal Nyquist

items.rationale: GS-FSC at 0.143 between independently refined half-maps is the field-standard resolution criterion.

items.scenario: A single 24 GB GPU is available and a non-uniform refinement of ~1.3M particles is required.
items.question: Reasonable approach?

options.A: Run sequentially on the single GPU; monitor memory and reduce batch if needed

items.rationale: Large refinements run on one GPU with sufficient VRAM (real run: ~14 GB used of 24 GB) given a sequential schedule.

items.scenario: A stretch target resolution is set below what the data can support so the adaptive loop keeps trying to improve.
items.question: What is the effect of an unreachable target with keep-best?

options.A: It ignores the data
options.C: The loop keeps exploring but never reports worse than baseline; it stops on its iteration budget

items.rationale: An unreachable target simply drives continued exploration bounded by max-iterations; keep-best keeps the reported result safe.

items.scenario: A dataset is a mixture of four different proteins imaged as single-frame micrographs (no movie frames).
items.question: Why is this unsuitable for a single-structure C1 movie pipeline?

options.A: No movies means no motion correction/polishing, and a 4-protein mixture violates the single-structure assumption

items.rationale: A single-structure movie pipeline needs multi-frame movies and a homogeneous target; a mixture without movies breaks both (real dataset we rejected).

items.scenario: 2D classification puts 80% of particles into 2 dominant classes and spreads the rest thinly across 50 classes.
items.question: What might this indicate?

options.A: Symmetry is wrong
options.B: Strong preferred orientation and/or many junk classes; inspect before selecting

items.rationale: Heavy concentration in a couple of classes often reflects preferred views or dominant junk; requires inspection before 3D.

items.scenario: A manuscript claims an adaptive-selection contribution, but the headline result was produced with the software's built-in fixed selection and no adaptive step.
items.question: What is the correct scientific response?

options.A: Publish as-is
options.B: Reconcile: run the adaptive method to produce the headline result, or clearly state which method produced which number
options.D: Claim the fixed method is adaptive

items.rationale: Claims must match evidence; the fix is to connect the claimed method to the reported result or disclose the discrepancy (the exact issue a reviewer flagged in this project).
